perturbation.original_text: The Customizations shall fulfill or exceed all of the functional, performance and other specifications described in the Program Materials and the documents prepared either by Bank of America or Supplier pursuant to this Agreement. as indicated below. No later than <<enter Customization Documents Delivery Date>>, Supplier shall deliver to Bank of America the documents described below for this Customization. The detailed specifications so delivered shall be incorporated herein when approved by Bank of America.
perturbation.changed_text: The Customizations shall fulfill or satisfy some of the functional, performance and other specifications described in the Program Materials and the documents prepared either by Bank of America or Supplier pursuant to this Agreement. as indicated below. No later than <<enter Customization Documents Delivery Date>>, Supplier shall deliver to Bank of America only some of the documents described below for this Customization. The high-level specifications so delivered shall be incorporated herein when approved by Bank of America.
perturbation.explanation: The terms 'fulfill or exceed' was changed to 'fulfill or satisfy some'. The term 'the documents' was changed to 'only some of the documents'. Finally, the term 'detailed specifications' was changed to 'high-level specifications'. These changes introduce ambiguity regarding the extent to which the customizations must meet the required specifications, potentially leading to disputes and unenforceability.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 2-313. Express Warranties by Affirmation, Promise, Description, Sample.
perturbation.law_explanation: By weakening the requirements for fulfilling specifications and delivering documentation, and by using less precise terminology like 'high-level' instead of 'detailed', the modified text undermines the creation of express warranties as defined by UCC § 2-313. This section states that any affirmation of fact or promise made by the seller to the buyer which relates to the goods and becomes part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the goods shall conform to the affirmation or promise. The new text does not guarantee conformance.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-313. Express Warranties by Affirmation, Promise, Description, Sample. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-313. Express Warranties by Affirmation, Promise, Description, Sample. (1) Express warranties by the seller are created as follows: (a) Any affirmation of fact or promise made by the seller to the buyer which relates to the goods and becomes part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the goods shall conform to the affirmation or promise. (b) Any description of the goods which is made part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the goods shall conform to the description. (c) Any sample or model which is made part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the whole of the goods shall conform to the sample or model. (2) It is not necessary to the creation of an express warranty that the seller use formal words such as "warrant" or "guarantee" or that he have a specific intention to make a warranty, but an affirmation merely of the value of the goods or a statement purporting to be merely the seller's opinion or commendation of the goods does not create a warranty. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity and weakens the assurance that the customizations will meet the required specifications. UCC § 2-313(1)(a) states that any affirmation or promise by the seller that relates to the goods creates an express warranty that the goods shall conform to the affirmation or promise. By changing 'fulfill or exceed all' to 'fulfill or satisfy some', and 'detailed specifications' to 'high-level specifications', the modified text no longer guarantees conformance with all specifications, thus contradicting the express warranty provision as the new text does not guarantee conformance.

perturbation.original_text: Fees for Customizations: [SPECIFY TIME AND MATERIALS RATES ([Indicate any "not to exceed" limit on T&M Customization costs) OR FIXED PRICE] If the proposed price Is Increased by Supplier upon delivery to Bank of America of detailed specifications or at any other time hereafter, Bank of America may terminate this Schedule and shall receive a refund of all amounts previously paid hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: Fees for Customizations: [SPECIFY TIME AND MATERIALS RATES ([Indicate any "not to exceed" limit on T&M Customization costs) OR FIXED PRICE] If the estimated price Is Increased by Supplier for unforeseen reasons or at any other time hereafter, Bank of America may negotiate new payment terms.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'proposed price' to 'estimated price' and replacing 'Bank of America may terminate this Schedule and shall receive a refund of all amounts previously paid hereunder' with 'Bank of America may negotiate new payment terms' introduces ambiguity and removes the clear right of termination and refund. This makes the financial terms less certain.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the definite price adjustment and refund mechanism, the modified text introduces an 'open price term' scenario but fails to adequately address the requirements under UCC § 2-305. This section requires good faith and a commercially reasonable standard in determining the price. By only stating 'negotiate new payment terms' with 'unforeseen reasons', the modified text makes it uncertain how the price will be ultimately determined and what is considered to be an 'unforeseen reason'.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-305. Open Price Term. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-305. Open Price Term. (1) The parties if they so intend can conclude a contract for sale even though the price is not settled. In such a case the price is a reasonable price at the time for delivery if (a) nothing is said as to price; or (b) the price is left to be agreed by the parties and they fail to agree; or (c) the price is to be fixed in terms of some agreed market or other standard as set or recorded by a third person or agency and it is not so set or recorded. (2) A price to be fixed by the seller or by the buyer means a price for him to fix in good faith . (3) When a price left to be fixed otherwise than by agreement of the parties fails to be fixed through fault of one party the other may at his option treat the contract as cancelled or himself fix a reasonable price. (4) Where, however, the parties intend not to be bound unless the price be fixed or agreed and it is not fixed or agreed there is no contract . In such a case the buyer must return any goods already received or if unable so to do must pay their reasonable value at the time of delivery and the seller must return any portion of the price paid on account. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a legal contradiction. The original text provided a clear termination right and refund for Bank of America if the price increased. The changed text replaces this with 'negotiate new payment terms,' introducing an open price term. According to UCC § 2-305, if parties intend to conclude a contract without a settled price, the price must be reasonable. The 'Law Explanation' indicates that the new text fails to adequately address the requirements under UCC § 2-305, specifically lacking good faith and commercial reasonableness standards in determining the price. By changing from a definite price adjustment to 'negotiate new payment terms', the modification contradicts the legal requirement for a reasonable and good faith price determination when dealing with open price terms, as mentioned in 'Scraped Law Snippet 1'.

perturbation.original_text: Unless otherwise specified herein, the environment for this Customization Is the same as described on Product License Schedule A to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Unless otherwise roughly estimated herein, the environment for this Customization Is generally similar to that described on Product License Schedule A to this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The terms 'specified' and 'same' are replaced with 'roughly estimated' and 'generally similar'. This introduces imprecision and uncertainty about the environment for the customization, potentially leading to disputes.
perturbation.law_explanation: If the customization involves systems covered by 21 CFR Part 11 (Electronic Records; Electronic Signatures), this change introduces compliance issues. This regulation requires precise and controlled environments. Using terms like 'roughly estimated' and 'generally similar' demonstrates a lack of rigor that is fundamentally at odds with the stringent requirements of Part 11, especially concerning system validation and accuracy of electronic records.

Electronic Signatures - Scope and Application Contains Nonbinding Recommendations This guidance represents the Food and Drug Administration's (FDA's) current thi
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces imprecise language ('roughly estimated,' 'generally similar') regarding the environment for customization. If the customization involves systems covered by 21 CFR Part 11, as the Law Explanation suggests, this imprecision creates a legal contradiction. Part 11 requires precise and controlled environments, which is inconsistent with vague estimates. The scraped snippets confirm that Part 11 pertains to Electronic Records and Electronic Signatures, and the FDA provides guidance on its scope and application, supporting the Law Explanation's claim. The modified text's move to vague estimates weakens the regulatory requirements and contradicts the need for accuracy and validation of electronic records, as implied by Part 11.
